modules.title: Introduction to MHS & the M-PNP Vision
modules.subtitle: The interoperability problem, the three-rung ladder, and the v0.5 specifications bundle.

slides.title: Introduction to MHS & the M-PNP Vision
slides.subtitle: The interoperability problem, the three-rung ladder, and the v0.5 specifications bundle.

slides.title: Four Modules, One Course
slides.subtitle: From the motivation through the technical core to the on-ramp for contributing back.
modules_listed: Introduction to MHS & the M-PNP Vision

slides.title: Plug-and-Code, Not Plug-and-Play
slides.subtitle: The hardware is interoperable. The firmware isn't.

bullets.text: electrically and mechanically — demonstrated at multiple OCP summits with cross-vendor SCMs and HPMs.
of the HPM, so vendors co-design or maintain a tree of per-HPM firmware images.
is the workstream's name for the result — bespoke loads that don't carry across HPM generations or vendors.
time-to-market for new HPMs, CapEx and OpEx to integrate and validate, lost sustainability, customer friction on BYO security.

figure.alt: DC-MHS HPM and SCM block diagram showing ROT, BMC, BIOS, LTPI, SCM CPLD components
figure.caption: A typical DC-MHS platform: SCM (top right) with BMC, BIOS, ROT and SCM CPLD; HPM (below) carrying the processor subsystems. Electrically interoperable, but firmware is co-designed per HPM.

slides.title: A Three-Rung Ladder to Plug-and-Play

figure.alt: From Plug-and-Code to Plug-and-Play three-rung diagram
figure.caption: Plug-and-Code (custom code per model) → Plug-and-Play Lite (some files dynamically loaded) → Plug-and-Play (runtime loading of all definition files; baseline functionality for any conforming HPM).

slides.caption: v0.5 of the M-PNP bundle lands roughly at Plug-and-Play Lite. v1.0 targets full Plug-and-Play.

slides.title: DC-MHS Family and M-PNP’s Place
slides.subtitle: M-PNP is the discovery and interoperability layer above this stack of hardware specs.

cards.title: M-FLW / M-DNO / M-SDNO
cards.body: HPM form-factor specs — full-width and density-optimized.

cards.title: M-CRPS
cards.body: Common Redundant Power Supply spec.

cards.title: M-PIC
cards.body: Platform Infrastructure Connectivity — power, control panel, cooling.

cards.title: M-XIO
cards.body: Extensible high-speed I/O connector & pinout.

cards.title: M-PESTI
cards.body: Peripheral Sideband Tunneling Interface — used for risers/backplanes.

cards.title: DC-SCM 2.1 + HPM Common Circuits Type 1
cards.body: Secure control module and standardized HPM circuits the SCM expects.

slides.title: The M-PNP Specifications Bundle v0.5
slides.subtitle: Three normative specs, two JSON schemas, eight worked examples.

bullets.text: the glue — vision, definitions, conformance, references (15 pp).
the operational core — FRU info, discovery, boot process (148 pp).
the standardized FPGA architecture (227 pp).
hardware-component v0.5.6 and fpga v0.1.3.
HPM Type 1, four risers, UBM backplane, Type 2 PDB — each as JSON + PDF rendering.

figure.caption: The bundle: Interop spec, FRU/Discovery/Boot spec, HPM FPGA spec, JSON schemas, and JSON mockups.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
items: M-PNP is not new hardware — it's a way of describing existing DC-MHS hardware so one BMC firmware image runs the whole ecosystem.
The unit of description is a JSON file the hardware vendor writes at design time; the BMC retrieves it at boot.
The bundle is layered: Interop sets the contract, FRU/Discovery/Boot operationalizes it, the FPGA spec gives the BMC a stable surface to talk to.
v0.5 lands at “Plug-and-Play Lite”; v1.0 (Q1 ’27) targets full Plug-and-Play.

slides.title: Check Your M-PNP Foundation
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: What is the central problem M-PNP is trying to remove from DC-MHS platforms?

choices.text: BMC firmware needing prior, model-specific knowledge of each HPM
The need for mechanically standardized module form factors
The use of field-replaceable components in server platforms
The requirement to use JSON anywhere in the design process

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. M-PNP lets the BMC discover the platform from self-describing data instead of relying on a per-HPM firmware image.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the plug-and-code discussion. The pain point is BMC firmware that already has to know the HPM model before boot.

questions.prompt: Which items belong to the M-PNP specifications bundle described in this module?

choices.text: M-PNP.INTEROP
M-PNP.FRU_DISCOVERY_BOOT
M-PNP.FPGA
JSON schemas and worked mockups
A new processor socket specification

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The bundle combines the Interop, FRU/Discovery/Boot, and FPGA specs with schemas and worked JSON examples.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Look back at the bundle slide. It layers three normative specs with schemas and examples; it is not a new processor socket spec.

slides.thank_you_message: Nice work — you’ve finished Module 1: Introduction to MHS & the M-PNP Vision.

modules.title: FRU, Discovery & Boot Specification
modules.subtitle: How a BMC learns the platform from a cold start.

slides.title: FRU, Discovery & Boot Specification
slides.subtitle: How a BMC learns the platform from a cold start.

slides.title: Learning Objectives
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module you will be able to…
objectives: Explain the three pillars of the specification — FRU information, discovery, and boot — and how they fit together.
Distinguish between local attributes stored on the component and extended attributes stored elsewhere.
Trace the breadth-first discovery process through its four phases.
Walk through HPM discovery with security gates and the FPGA power states involved.
Identify the role of MMBI in the BMC-to-BIOS handoff during early boot.

slides.title: Three Pillars
slides.subtitle: The 148 pages rest on three load-bearing ideas.

bullets.text: Every component (HPM + every peripheral) exposes a FRU information device with a known, parsable record.
The BMC walks the platform in a deterministic, breadth-first order and builds a complete topology before the BIOS runs.
Boot is interleaved with discovery — attestation gates occur at specific power-state checkpoints.

slides.title: Three Layers of HPM FRU Information

figure.alt: Three-layer FRU information stack: IPMI multi-record, DMTF DSP0220 FRU + cert + signature, Extended JSON
figure.caption: Layer 1: IPMI multi-record on EEPROM at I2C bus 0 / address 0xA0. Layer 2: DMTF DSP0220 FRU + optional cert + signature. Layer 3: extended JSON (large, may live on EEPROM, virtual EEPROM in FPGA, or BIOS firmware volume).

slides.caption: The File Descriptor table inside the DMTF FRU points the BMC to the local + extended JSON. The Resident bit + Context field tell it where to look.

slides.title: Breadth-First Discovery, Four Phases

figure.alt: Breadth-first discovery diagram — SCM to HPM to risers/backplanes/PDBs to endpoint peripherals
figure.caption: Phase 0: SCM init. Phase 1: HPM. Phase 2: non-endpoint peripherals (risers, backplanes, PDBs, control panels). Phase 3: endpoint peripherals (NICs, drives, OCP CEM cards, M-CRPS supplies).

slides.caption: Each ring's JSON tells the BMC how to interpret the next ring. Boot is interleaved but most of discovery completes before the BIOS gets to run.

slides.title: HPM Discovery Walkthrough
slides.subtitle: From pre-standby to a validated HPM description in memory.

bullets.text: BMC reads IPMI FRU from I2C bus 0 / 0xA0. Optionally validates digital cert + signature; optionally runs SPDM.
SCM asserts SCM_HPM_STBY_EN → BMC moves to Standby Hold-Off. HPM asserts HPM_SCM_STBY_RDY when aux rails regulate.
Security gate — BMC attests the HPM FPGA while it is powered but held in reset.
SCM de-asserts SCM_HPM_STBY_RST_N → FPGA exits reset → Standby Run.
BMC retrieves local + extended HPM JSON via PLDM File Transfer or the File Descriptor table in the DMTF FRU.

figure.alt: Early HPM Discovery block diagram showing BMC+SCM CPLD, JTAG/I2C/I3C, FPGA, EEPROM/FRU paths
figure.caption: Figure 6 from the FRU/Discovery/Boot spec. Power signals, JTAG, I2C/I3C, SCI, SPDM/MCTP paths from SCM to HPM FPGA.

slides.title: Six Concrete Peripheral Mockups
slides.subtitle: The bundle ships worked examples for the most common non-endpoint peripherals.

cards.title: Riser · 1-slot x8
cards.body: Single PCIe CEM x8 slot riser. peripherals-fru-riser-1-x8-slot v0.3.4.

cards.title: Riser · 1-slot x16
cards.body: Single PCIe CEM x16 slot riser. v0.3.1.

cards.title: Riser · 2-slot x8
cards.body: Two-slot PCIe CEM x8 riser. v0.3.1.

cards.title: Riser · 2-slot x16
cards.body: Two-slot PCIe CEM x16 riser. v0.3.1.

cards.title: UBM Backplane
cards.body: Universal Backplane Management for NVMe drives. v0.3.5.

cards.title: PDB · Type 2
cards.body: Power Distribution Board. peripherals-fru-pdb-type2 v0.1.5.

slides.title: Ten Bus Description Blocks
slides.subtitle: Every component publishes the same shape of JSON, bus by bus. One parser handles the whole system.

bullets.text: Multiplexer tree, device addresses, speed mode.
Lane assignments and bifurcation options.
Tunneled signals per target.
Channel / bit mappings to virtual wires.
Endpoint topology and role.
Every physical connector and which buses pass through it.

slides.title: Boot Process & BMC↔BIOS via MMBI
slides.subtitle: Discovery dovetails into boot through the FPGA power state machine.

bullets.bold_lead: S0 Run-BIOS-Boot exists for a reason
bullets.text: PCIe devices can't be attested until link training; this state lets the BMC attest over the device's OOB interface before the OS runs.
BMC and BIOS must implement DMTF DSP0282 v1.0 Memory-Mapped Buffer Interface for early-boot communication.
MMBI carries a request-response protocol over shared memory; specific payloads are still being defined in v0.5.
Only after every runtime-attested peripheral signs off.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
items: FRU info lives in three layers: IPMI multi-record, DMTF FRU (signable), JSON (deep self-description).
Discovery is breadth-first across four phases (SCM → HPM → non-endpoint peripherals → endpoints).
Security gates are baked into the process — every tier is attested before it leaves Hold-Off.
The bundle ships six concrete peripheral mockups so you can read schema before writing code.
BMC ↔ BIOS early-boot communication runs on DMTF MMBI; the FPGA power state machine drives the choreography.

slides.title: Check Your Discovery Model
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: Which sequence best matches the breadth-first discovery process?

choices.text: SCM initialization → HPM → non-endpoint peripherals → endpoint peripherals
Endpoint peripherals → non-endpoint peripherals → HPM → SCM initialization
BIOS boot → OS handoff → HPM JSON retrieval → SCM initialization
Risers and backplanes first, then the HPM, then FRU storage

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Discovery moves outward from the SCM to the HPM, then to intermediate peripherals, then to endpoints.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the four-phase discovery diagram. The BMC starts with the SCM/HPM path before it walks peripherals and endpoints.

questions.prompt: What role does MMBI play in the boot process covered here?

choices.text: It provides BMC-to-BIOS early-boot communication over a shared memory interface.
It replaces the component FRU information device.
It stores every peripheral JSON file inside the FPGA memory map.
It performs PCIe link training on behalf of BIOS.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. MMBI is the required BMC-to-BIOS communication mechanism during early boot.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Look back at the boot slide. MMBI is a request-response communication path; it does not replace FRU data or train PCIe links.

slides.thank_you_message: Nice work — you’ve finished Module 2: FRU, Discovery & Boot Specification.

modules.title: M-PNP HPM FPGA Specification
modules.subtitle: A standardized API for the HPM FPGA.

slides.title: M-PNP HPM FPGA Specification
slides.subtitle: A standardized API for the HPM FPGA — IP blocks, memory map, virtual wires, power states.

slides.title: Learning Objectives
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module you will be able to…
objectives: Explain the FPGA standardization concept and where vendor / standardized / integrator logic each sit.
Enumerate the FPGA’s outward and inward common interfaces.
List the 13 IP blocks in the catalog and identify which 6 are required.
Describe the FPGA memory map and the three transports the BMC can use to access it.
Walk all 10 power states with their attestation hold-off gates.
Explain the virtual wires framework and the FPGA self-describing JSON.

slides.title: A Layered Architecture

figure.alt: HPM FPGA standardized architecture diagram — vendor logic on top, MHS-standardized IP in middle, integrator logic on bottom
figure.caption: HPM Vendor customizations and System Integrator custom logic sit above and below a stable layer of MHS-standardized building blocks (LTPI, M-PESTI, Power Mgmt, Memory Map CSRs, Virtual Wires, BMC Interfaces).

slides.caption: The spec dictates the API, not the implementation. No max LUT count, no required memory size — just the CSRs, the memory map, the virtual wires, and the JSON that describes all of them.

slides.title: Outward and Inward

figure.alt: FPGA common interfaces diagram showing LTPI, SGPIO, SPI, I2C/I3C and JTAG to SCM, plus M-PESTI, FlexIO, DSSI and SGPIO inside HPM
figure.caption: Outward (to SCM): LTPI, SGPIO, SPI, I2C/I3C, JTAG. Inward (to HPM): M-PESTI, M-XIO FlexIO, M-PIC sideband, DSSI, SGPIO to PDB.

slides.caption: Normative requirement (§8): the FPGA shall support virtual-wire access plus memory-mapped access using either SGPIO + SPI/I2C/I3C, or LTPI for both.

slides.title: 13 IP Blocks — 6 Required, 7 Optional
slides.subtitle: From Power Sequencing to General GPIOs.
rows: Unified HPM power states + transition-request CSRs
Virtual reset for MXIO, OCP slots, on-board devices
Maps FPGA internal signals to SGPIO/LTPI transport
CSR mapping for peripheral identification (DSTAT min.)
Configurable IO pins on M-XIO + M-PIC sideband

slides.title: Directory + Required + Optional + OEM
slides.subtitle: The BMC reads the directory first to learn where everything else lives.

bullets.text: Header listing every section and its offset.
Standardized IP blocks the spec mandates.
Defined by the spec but not required.
Vendor / integrator-specific content.
SPI (random access), LTPI Data Channel (multiplexed), I2C/I3C mailbox (slower, always-present in standby).

figure.alt: FPGA Memory Map Architecture diagram — interface CSRs, directory, required sections, optional sections, OEM sections
figure.caption: Interface CSRs sit at the top, followed by the Required Directory, then required/optional/OEM sections. The same content is reachable from any of the three transports.

slides.title: 10 States, 4 Hold-Offs, 3 Attestation Gates

figure.alt: FPGA Power State Transition Diagram — G3 through S0 Run-OS with hold-off variants
figure.caption: Figure 15 of the FPGA spec. G3 → Pre-STBY → STBY (Hold-Off) → STBY (Run) → S5 (Hold-Off) → S5 (Run) → S0 (SoC Ramp) → S0 (Hold-Off) → S0 (Run-BIOS-Boot) → S0 (Run-OS). The four Hold-Off states are where the BMC attests each tier of devices before releasing them.

slides.caption: Three attestation gates fire in Standby Hold-Off (FPGA), S5 Hold-Off (FRU storage devices), and S0 Hold-Off / Run-BIOS-Boot (SoCs and PCIe devices). Security policy is up to the integrator; the state machine just makes the gates possible.

slides.title: Logical Signals on a Physical Transport
slides.subtitle: Thousands of internal FPGA signals reach the BMC without a pin-per-signal connector.

bullets.text: Spec defines required + optional virtual wires (PWRGD, reset requests, interrupts, status bits, GPIOs).
Each virtual wire maps to a bit position in an SGPIO frame or an LTPI virtual wire channel.
Vendor publishes the mapping in fpga JSON; BMC reads it at discovery time.
Vendors add additional virtual wires for their own signals; BMC ingests via the same JSON.
Same plumbing also handles FPGA package-pin GPIO mapping.

slides.title: fpga Schema v0.1.3 — What the BMC Reads
slides.subtitle: An FPGA build emits a JSON file that describes itself exactly.

bullets.text: Vendor, version, build identifier.
Required ones from the catalog + any vendor-defined blocks.
Register name, offset, access type, bit fields, reset values.
Section names and address ranges.
Every logical signal ↔ transport-bit pairing.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
items: The standardization concept layers vendor logic, MHS-standardized IP, and integrator logic so each can change without breaking the others.
The FPGA shall support virtual wires + memory-mapped access — via SGPIO + SPI/I2C/I3C or LTPI for both.
13 IP blocks; 6 required. Vendor-defined blocks must also publish a self-describing JSON.
Memory map: directory + required + optional + OEM. Accessible via SPI, LTPI Data Channel, or I2C/I3C mailbox.
10 power states with 4 hold-off variants — the BMC attests each tier before releasing reset.
FPGA self-describing JSON tells the BMC exactly what's in this build, so one BMC firmware image can run any conforming HPM.

slides.title: Check Your FPGA API Model
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: What does the M-PNP HPM FPGA specification standardize?

choices.text: The API surface: CSRs, memory map, virtual wires, IP block descriptions, and JSON self-description
A maximum LUT count and one required FPGA vendor implementation
Only the physical package pinout of the FPGA device
Only the BMC firmware update sequence

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The spec standardizes the interface the BMC can rely on, not the vendor implementation details.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the layered architecture slide. The spec defines the stable API surface, not a required LUT count or single implementation.

questions.prompt: Which concepts are tied to the FPGA power-state and attestation model?

choices.text: Hold-Off states where hardware is powered but reset is held
The BMC attesting each tier before releasing it
A transition to S0 Run-OS only after required runtime attestation completes
Skipping discovery once main power is available

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Hold-Off states create the checkpoints the BMC uses before releasing hardware into the next stage.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the power-state diagram. The point is controlled release after attestation, not skipping discovery.

slides.thank_you_message: Nice work — you’ve finished Module 3: M-PNP HPM FPGA Specification.

modules.title: Interop, Conformance, Tooling & Adoption
modules.subtitle: The Interop spec, the schemas, the tools, the roadmap, and how to contribute.

slides.title: Interop, Conformance, Tooling & Adoption
slides.subtitle: Bring it home: the Interop spec, the JSON ecosystem, the tools, and the roadmap.

slides.title: Learning Objectives
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module you will be able to…
objectives: Describe what the Interop spec adds beyond the FRU and FPGA specs.
Identify the JSON schemas and mockup examples in the v0.5 bundle.
Use the open-source tools (frupack.py shipped, fpga2json.py coming Q2 ’26).
Locate the workstream in the v0.5 → v1.0 timeline.
Know exactly how to contribute back.

slides.title: The Interoperability Specification (v0.3.0)
slides.subtitle: Fifteen pages of contract. Intentionally short.

bullets.text: What interoperability means — mechanically, electrically, logically.
Plug-and-Code → Plug-and-Play Lite → Baseline Plug-and-Play.
Common requirements that span the bundle.
What a supplier must meet for OCP recognition.
FRU/Discovery/Boot, FPGA, DC-SCM 2.1, HPM Common Circuits, OCP NIC, DMTF (FRU/PLDM/MCTP/MMBI), PCIe, SPDM.

figure.alt: Interop spec content map — scope, vision, requirements, conformance, references
figure.caption: The Interop spec is the table of contents for everything else. Read it first.

cards.title: hardware-component v0.5.6
cards.body: Schema for HPMs and peripherals.

cards.title: fpga v0.1.3
cards.body: Schema for FPGAs and other PLDs.

cards.title: HPM Type 1 v0.6.8
cards.body: Complete worked example with 181 FPGA signals.

cards.title: FPGA mockup v0.1.13
cards.body: Paired with the HPM example.

cards.title: Riser · 1×x8
cards.body: Single-slot PCIe CEM x8.

cards.title: Riser · 1×x16 / 2×x8 / 2×x16
cards.body: Three more riser variants.

cards.title: UBM Backplane v0.3.5
cards.body: NVMe drive backplane.

cards.title: PDB Type 2 v0.1.5
cards.body: Power Distribution Board.

slides.title: frupack.py Today, fpga2json.py Next
slides.subtitle: Open-source tools for generating the artefacts the BMC reads.

cards.title: frupack.py — shipped
cards.body: FRU Packaging Tool, contributed by HPE. Takes a JSON description and packages it into binary FRU bytes with multi-record framing, offsets, and CRCs.

cards.title: fpga2json.py — Q2 ’26
cards.body: FPGA-to-JSON generator. Reads an FPGA build and produces the self-describing JSON the BMC ingests. HPE-contributed, targeted second quarter of 2026.

cards.title: Still needed
cards.body: JSON-from-schematic generators, JSON validators, BMC-side ingestion libraries. Live demos at OCP Innovation Stations at recent summits.

slides.title: v0.5 → v0.7 → v0.9 → v1.0

figure.alt: M-PNP Workstream Progress and Schedule timeline showing v0.5 to v1.0 milestones
figure.caption: v0.5 (Sept '25) shipped. v0.7 (Aug '26): FRU/Disc/Boot complete, FPGA IP defs complete, peripheral PLD reqs defined. v0.9 (~Oct '26 Global Summit): Interop spec complete. v1.0 (Q1 '27): finalize all specs and collaterals.

slides.caption: Every version increment is gated on community feedback and prototype validation. Engage during v0.7 / v0.9 to influence v1.0.

slides.title: Open Challenges & How to Help
slides.subtitle: What the workstream is wrestling with, and where new contributors land first.

bullets.text: LED policy, thermal envelopes — not on the HPM, not on the BMC. Where do they live?
OCP NIC + PCIe CEM cover a lot; the workstream is finding gaps to fill (often in those specs, not M-PNP).
Need FPGA + BMC + BIOS prototypes to validate the specs end-to-end.
DC-MHS wiki, public mailing list ocp-mhs-public@OCP-All.groups.io, Tech Talks, Innovation Station demos.
Use frupack.py to package a JSON for hardware you care about — then bring questions.

slides.title: Check Your Adoption Path
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: What does the Interoperability specification add beyond the FRU and FPGA specifications?

choices.text: The cross-spec contract: vision, definitions, common requirements, conformance criteria, and references
A replacement for all endpoint standards such as OCP NIC and PCIe CEM
A mandatory production BMC codebase
A new chassis-only JSON schema that is already complete in v0.5

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The Interop spec is the short cross-spec contract that ties the bundle together.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the Interop slide. It does not replace endpoint standards or ship a mandatory BMC codebase.

questions.prompt: Which tooling statement matches this module?

choices.text: frupack.py packages JSON descriptions into binary FRU bytes; fpga2json.py is planned next.
fpga2json.py is the only shipped tool in the bundle today.
frupack.py replaces the need for schema validation or worked examples.
The tooling is unrelated to the data the BMC reads.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. frupack.py is the current packaging tool, while fpga2json.py is the planned generator for FPGA self-description.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the tooling slide. frupack.py exists today; fpga2json.py is the planned next step.

slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing Module 4: Interop, Conformance, Tooling & Adoption.
slides.cert_message: You've completed every module of the OCP M-PNP Academy course. Visit opencompute.org/wiki/Server/DC-MHS to plug into the workstream.

survey.body: Got a couple minutes to help us improve? Help us shape improvements and our future courses by sharing your feedback with our development team. We periodically select submitters at random for great giveaways!

term_glossary.tooltip: Modular Plug-and-Play; the MHS layer that lets BMC firmware discover and operate conforming hardware from self-describing data.
Data Center Modular Hardware System; OCP's modular server hardware family that M-PNP builds on.
Host Processor Module; the compute module containing the processor complex and platform logic.
Secure Control Module; the management and control module that carries the BMC, root of trust, firmware storage, and platform control functions.
Baseboard Management Controller; the management processor that discovers hardware, reads FRU data, and coordinates platform boot.
Field Replaceable Unit; a replaceable component that exposes identity and descriptive information to platform management firmware.
Legacy FRU information format used for basic component identification and inventory records.
DMTF FRU data format used for richer FRU descriptions, including structures M-PNP can use for signed metadata and file descriptors.
Security Protocol and Data Model; a DMTF protocol used for device authentication, measurement, and attestation flows.
Platform Level Data Model; a DMTF management protocol family used here as one access path for file transfer and platform data exchange.
Modular Peripheral Sideband Tunneling Interface; the sideband tunneling path used for peripheral discovery and control in DC-MHS systems.
LVDS Tunneling Protocol and Interface; a DC-SCM sideband transport for carrying low-pin-count management signals.
Memory-Mapped Buffer Interface; DMTF DSP0282 interface for BMC-to-BIOS communication during early boot.
Field-Programmable Gate Array; the programmable logic device that exposes M-PNP's standardized HPM control surface.
Control/status register; a memory-mapped register the BMC reads or writes to inspect state or request actions.
A reusable FPGA function block with defined behavior, registers, and self-description.
Logical sideband signals carried over transports like SGPIO or LTPI instead of dedicated physical pins.
A powered-but-held-in-reset state that gives the BMC a checkpoint for attestation before releasing the next tier of hardware.
Universal Backplane Management; a backplane management framework used with NVMe and other storage backplane designs.
Power Distribution Board; a board that distributes platform power and can publish FRU and JSON data.
